voiceover: You were told you were too sensitive, too scattered, too much. But what if it was never a personality flaw? Seven signs of ADHD in women that get dismissed constantly. Emotional meltdowns over small things — that's rejection sensitivity, not drama. Losing track of conversations mid-sentence — that's working memory, not rudeness. Exhaustion from just existing — that's your brain working overtime to mask. Hyperfocusing on one thing and forgetting everything else — not passion, executive dysfunction. Missing deadlines despite caring deeply — not laziness, time blindness. Chronic self-blame for all of it — that's a system that missed you, not a character flaw. You were never the problem. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 7 Signs of ADHD in Women That Get Dismissed #Shorts
description: ADHD in women looks different — and it gets missed for decades. These 7 signs are often dismissed as personality traits, sensitivity, or laziness, but they are rooted in neuroscience. If you saw yourself in this, you are not broken, you were just never given the right framework. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Your brain won't start tasks? Stop forcing it. Bribe it. A dopamine menu is a short list of tiny rewards you pick from before or during hard tasks. Step one: write down five to ten things that give your brain a quick hit. Coffee, a playlist, a snack, a candle. Step two: before you sit down to work, pick one and have it ready. Step three: pair it with the task every single time. Your brain starts linking the reward to the work. That's not laziness. That's how dopamine actually works. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Use a Dopamine Menu to Finally Start Tasks #Shorts
description: If your ADHD brain refuses to start hard tasks, a dopamine menu can help you build momentum by pairing small rewards with work. This technique works with your brain's dopamine system instead of against it. Try it today and see the difference a tiny bribe makes. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: You thought you were lazy. Disorganized. Dramatic. But these are actually ADHD symptoms. Forgetting words mid-sentence — that's working memory, not stupidity. Feeling exhausted after doing nothing — that's your brain burning energy just to stay regulated. Saying yes when you mean no because you can't think fast enough to refuse — that's impulsive compliance. Hating loud sounds or scratchy tags — that's sensory sensitivity, common in ADHD. And that crushing guilt you feel constantly — that's emotional dysregulation, not weakness. None of this is a character flaw. Sound familiar every single day? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: 5 ADHD Symptoms You Didn't Know Were ADHD #Shorts
description: Think you're just lazy or too sensitive? These 5 hidden ADHD symptoms are commonly misread as personality flaws. From word-finding struggles to emotional dysregulation, ADHD shows up in ways most people never expect. Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Out of sight, out of mind — but for ADHD brains, that is literally true. Object permanence means if you can't see it, your brain stops registering it exists. Here's what it looks like: You ignore a text for days — it just vanished from your brain. You forget laundry in the machine until it smells. You lose your keys on the counter in plain sight. You meant to reply to that email. Your brain filed it under gone. This is not laziness. It's how your brain handles what's not in front of it. Common in ADHD, and completely valid. Does this sound like you? Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: ADHD Object Permanence: Out of Sight Out of Mind #Shorts
description:
If something is out of sight, your ADHD brain may genuinely stop registering it exists — that's object permanence, and it's a real neurological pattern, not a character flaw. From forgotten texts to keys hiding in plain sight, this is one of the most relatable ADHD experiences there is. Common in ADHD, completely valid, and now you have a name for it.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

question: Which inbox makes you sweat most?

options.label: The avalanche
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. A cozy illustrated laptop screen glowing softly, with a towering overflowing stack of envelopes spilling out of the screen like a avalanche, envelopes scattered across a warm cream desk, a small wilting plant nearby, gentle chaos everywhere.
options.result: You're drowning in volume - every unread message feels like a tiny emergency alarm.

options.label: The one email
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy illustrated laptop screen glowing softly on a tidy desk, showing a single solitary envelope sitting alone in a vast empty space, a small spotlight effect around it, a tiny wilted flower beside the laptop, the emptiness around it feeling weirdly ominous.
options.result: One unanswered thing haunts you more than a thousand - your brain loops on the unfinished.

options.label: The bold names
options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. Palette: lavender, cream, sage green, blush pink. Calm, friendly, approachable, non-judgmental mood. No people, no faces, no text, no letters. Vertical 9:16 composition. A cozy illustrated open notebook on a warm desk with a list of items, some with small bold star symbols beside them and some plain, a gentle glow highlighting the starred ones, a soft cup of tea nearby, the contrast between marked and unmarked items feeling quietly stressful.
options.result: Urgency cues are your kryptonite - bold and flagged means your nervous system is already activated.

options.imagePrompt: Hand-drawn illustration in soft pastel colors, cozy and warm style. Soft watercolor texture, gentle hand-painted lines, flat illustration. options.result: You don't have an inbox problem - you have an ADHD nervous system that treats all input as urgent.

title: Which inbox stresses you out most? (ADHD test) #Shorts
description:
Pick the inbox that makes you sweat - then watch as we reveal what each choice says about your ADHD brain, one option at a time. This one is uncomfortably accurate.
Follow for daily ADHD content.

voiceover: Someone gives you mild feedback and your whole body floods with shame. That is not you being dramatic. That is rejection sensitive dysphoria, and it is one of the most painful hidden symptoms of ADHD. Here is the science. ADHD brains are wired differently around emotional regulation. The part of your brain that buffers emotional signals, the prefrontal cortex, has weaker connections to your limbic system. So when rejection hits, there is no filter. The pain arrives at full volume, instantly. It feels like the worst moment of your life, even if it was just a comment on your work. And here is what makes it harder: you cannot logic your way out of it in the moment because the emotional brain has already taken the wheel. So what actually helps? First, name it in real time. Say out loud, this is RSD, not reality. Second, give yourself twenty minutes before you respond or decide anything. The intensity will drop. Third, tell someone you trust about your RSD so they understand why you sometimes go quiet or spiral. You are not too sensitive. Your brain just has a louder alarm. Follow for daily ADHD content.

title: Why Criticism Physically Hurts With ADHD #Shorts
description:
Rejection sensitive dysphoria is one of the most painful and least talked about symptoms of ADHD. If criticism hits you like a wave of total shame, this is the neuroscience behind why, and three things that actually help. Common in ADHD, and finally explainable.
Follow for daily ADHD content.
